speaker: interviewer
text: It's Sunday, May 5, 1996. This is an interview with a survivor, Joseph Weinreich. The interviewer is Ian Fischl. We are in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. And this is an English language interview.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is May 5, 1996. The survivor's name is Mr. Joseph Weinreich. My name is Ian Fischl. The city is Sydney. The state is New South Wales. The country, Australia. And the language is English. The surname is spelled W-E-I-N-R-E-I-C-H. The interviewer's surname is F-I-S-C-H-L. Can you please tell us your name, and also spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Joseph Weinreich. The spelling is W-E-I-N-R-E-I-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other names that you were known by?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 24th of the second, '27.

speaker: interviewer
text: 24th of February?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 24th of February, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your age?

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us, and also spell it, the name of the city and country where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the city is Chrzanow, C-H-R-Z-A-N-O-W. And the country is Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Chrzanow located?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chrzanow was located in the south of Poland, and about an hour and a half, two hours, from the Czech border. And the nearest city, which was about 3/4 of an hour away-- significant city-- was Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly. My father's name was Chaim Weinreich. But he was not born in Chrzanow. He was born in Bochnia. And my mother's name was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Bochnia for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bochnia, B-O-C-H-N-I-A. And my mother's name was Friedl. And her maiden name was Schreiber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-C-H-R-E-I-B-E-R. And I had a brother whose name was Moshe, and he was seven years older than I. This was the nearest family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uncles, aunts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. First, do you want the grandfathers first?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two sets of grandparents. One set of grandparents was the Weinreich, which lived in Bochnia, which I hardly knew because there were about 120, 130 kilometers away. And in those days 130 kilometers was a long way. And the Schreibers, which was on my mother's side, and my grandfather's name was Simcha. And my grandmother's name was Malka. And we lived in the same block of flats-- which belonged to my grandfather-- opposite each other. So I knew this family-- the Schreiber family, I knew extremely well. Simcha Schreiber had five sons and two daughters. Now the eldest daughter was Friedl, that was my mother. And then you had Abys, Bernard, Yitzrik, Lizer, Mendel, and my mother's name, I said, Friedl. And my auntie's name was Minda-- Mindel-- and Mina. And they were all married. And they all had two children, practically, each. Every one of them had two children each.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a very large family.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a large family, but there's two of them-- two of my uncles did not live in Chrzanow. One lived in Palestine. He left Poland somewhere around 1928, '29. And my other uncle, Lizer-- which his name here in Australia, was Lester-- he lived in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was also married and had three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a merchant in leather. When he married my mother, it was a [NON-ENGLISH]. In those days there were [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, there was a [NON-ENGLISH]. And so when he married my mother, he came to live in Chrzanow. And from home, his parents, his father, and his family, were also in leather.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, if I may say so, can I speak something about my family in Bochnia? Now my grandfather Weinreich, Abraham Weinreich, started off an industry in Poland which exists still today. And they were making what they call [NON-ENGLISH]. It comes from the city Zakopane, in the mountains. It's a holiday resort.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jackets from lamb's leather, the lamb's wool, lamb's skins, and embroidered, for men, ladies, and also for children. And so he came from this background. And in Chrzanow, he started off a business, wholesale and retail of leather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a successful a business?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a successful business, but it was not a big business. It was a-- you see, my father had a completely different attitude to life, than say, the Schreiber family. For him, business was only a means to make a living. And but he was a scholar. And therefore, his business, he did not-- he put effort into it, of course. Otherwise he couldn't make a living. But it was not the whole-- it wasn't-- the business was not his whole life. He had different interests. And I'll tell you about those interests whenever you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as far as the religious background of your family, can you describe to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly, certainly. Shall I start with my father, or grandfather?

speaker: interviewer
text: You can start with your grandfather.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Now my grandfather was a Hungarian Jew. He was born in a city on the border in Poland, [NON-ENGLISH]. We were there anyway. After the war, we visited. And he came from an extremely religious family. And the name is Schreiber, right? Schreiber means writer, poet, writer. We can trace the Schreiber family right up to Hasam Sova Schreiber, which was the foremost rabbi at that time in Middle Europe. This man had a Yeshiva in Frankfurt-am-Main with 600 pupils. And he wrote a lot of books on Jewish law and so forth. And he's very well known in the-- in the Hasidic Jewish world, as the most important man of his time. And this went through from generation, to generation, to generation, until it came to my grandfather, which I knew so very well. My grandfather was a very religious man. He was a Jew with a long beard. And he was wearing a kittel in the Yom Tovim. He was wearing a [NON-ENGLISH], with a shtreimel And he brought up the children the same way. And he was an extremely religious man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was also, if I may say something-- speak about him now before I speak about my father-- that he was the representative of the Jewish community, in his little city where we lived. And on top of it, he was also a very able, and a very successful businessman. He was called the King of Wheat in Poland. He and his three sons and his son-in-law, were in business together, buying and selling wheat through all of Europe. And also they were selling food for carps, you know? I know in this country carps is a terrible thing, but in Poland it was a staple. It was like kartoffel, you know? And no Jew had Shabbos without carp. And so they were selling food for these lakes, you know? So he was a very successful man, and a very, very religious man. And they also gave him-- the owner, on the second day of Rosh Hashanah, he and his sons, whoever was in Poland at the time, you know, was davening the musaf service in the synagogue. And the sons were, of course, singing with him together, you know? And it was a tremendous thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'll tell you something about the Jewish life which we led in there that's unbelievable. Now my father came also from a very religious family in Bochnia. Also, they were married. It was intermarriage there, between rabbis' daughters, and children, and so forth. So my father came also from the same sort of background, a very, very religious background. So they were very compatible. My father fitted in very nicely with the whole lot. But he did not want to go in his grand-- in his father-in-law's business, because he wanted independence. And he saw-- this is what I can imagine now. He saw how intense they were working in their business. And I don't think he felt that type of life was good for him. So he had his own parnuse. He had his own shop. He had a wholesale business in leather.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what sort of languages would you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close to your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my grandfather I was extremely close, extremely close. First of all, we lived next door to one another. And you see, I used to go to cheder. And every Shabbat, in the morning, before we went to go to synagogue, it was time for [NON-ENGLISH]. What means [NON-ENGLISH] is it comes from the word, to hear, to listen. The grandfather used to ask me, what actually I learned in the cheder during the week, pasha of the week. And it was every Saturday morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then grandmother, Malka, used to come in after, I was a good boy, with the milk, and also with cake, like cheesecake, and poppy cake. And I love it till today, of course. So and grandfather took a tremendous interest in his grandchildren. And I don't-- maybe I imagine, and maybe not, but he was extremely fond of me, extremely, and so was my grandmother, Malka. Now next question. What do you want to ask me? What do you remember of the festivals and the religious life that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Festival life. Before I tell you about the festivals in my family, let me just give you a little description of the city. Chrzanow was a small city. You could call it a big shtetl, you know? It was as small city. It was only 30,000 inhabitants. Out of the 30,000 inhabitants, there was about 4,000 or 5,000 Polish people, and the rest were Jewish. So as I used to say, in Chrzanow, you don't need a calendar because you know when it's Friday night, when it's a Shabbos, when it's Yom Tovim. And the Yom Tovim were very-- first of all, every Friday night, it was a tremendous thing. The whole family used to go together to the synagogue. All [NON-ENGLISH], beautifully dressed in the [NON-ENGLISH], that's a certain coats, with the gartels, with the shtreimel. And the children were all beautifully dressed and going to the synagogue. I came back from the synagogue. I always remember my father used to walk in, and with always a song. I would like my children to know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you give us some that now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the tone, to get a flavor of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you recite something for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to walk in and he says, [SINGING NON-ENGLISH]. That's how he used to walk in. And it was festive. It was beautiful. The table was set on white cloth with the-- the candles were already on the table before Shabbos, of course. Not like here. And the food was very-- and the aroma.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of foods did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: All traditional food. What's the name? Soup, fish, meats, two or three different kinds of meats, [NON-ENGLISH], cake, whatever. That was the traditional thing. It was very beautiful. Beautifully prepared, and there was such a festivity. And at the table, we were singing the Shabbos songs. It was something incredible.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, you must understand, I've been away from home, and already now what, 60 years. What am I saying, 60 years, over 50 years, isn't it? Since 1939, and I still remember it till today. I still remember it. They used to sing.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Sweet, sweet. And when I sing this, I smell the soup with the [NON-ENGLISH], you know? And what was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine how deep it is in me, that I still remember that melody from home? Because so many years have passed. You mentioned that you have a brother as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's older, or younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was seven years older than me. His name was Moshe. Now I had a tremendous affection for my grandmother, of course, from my father, from my mother. But for him I had a special affection. Because, first of all, he was seven years older than me. And I remember the first time when I went to school. He said, anyone touches my brother, and I'll kill him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then it was this little-- what's the name-- deli shop around the corner where we lived. And he opened up account in there. And I could take out as much as I wanted up to one grosh, you know? And he-- and I was so proud of it, because I used to go with my friends. I said, look, I've got an account in here. Who had an account in a deli shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: And on top of it, he was a big boy. He was a big fellow. And he was seven years older than me, and very, religious of course, like all of us. I remember him so well because when it came to Rosh Hashanah--

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, Chrzanow had two synagogues, main two synagogues. It was 30,000 people there, 28,000, 29,000 people. So not everybody could fit into those synagogues. So therefore, they had dozens of shtiebel A shtiebel means a place, a small place where people congregate. It could be even their home where they lived. Right? And they davened and they prayed in there. And my brother used to go-- on Rosh Hashanah, he used to go and blow the shofar at those places. And I as a small boy, used to tag along. I used to carry a shofar and tallit. Of course, he's not allowed to-- he was not allowed to carry, right? And I carry it. And I always, always remember how he blew the shofar, because, you know, before you have the shofar [INAUDIBLE], you've got six or seven sentences, which the fellow-- the person who blows the shofar, has got to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until today, I remember. Especially one sentence, because he said it so beautifully, that it just stuck in my mind for all those years. He used to say, [SINGING NON-ENGLISH] And then he used to say, [IMITATING SOUND OF HORN] It was magnificent! I have a shofar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I don't remember his bar mitzvah. See, because when he has bar mitzvahed, he was 13. So how old was I? 6 or something, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Life in Chrzanow was like a traditional shtetl. Can you tell us a bit about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'll tell a bit about Chrzanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how people went about their daily activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. First of all, the whole thing revolved around the village. I made a couple of notes in here, what the city actually looked like, right? So when you ask me this question, if I may, I'll just read you just a couple of things. So that will give you a really good idea what Chrzanow was all about, all right? Chrzanow, as I said before, was a small city. It had 30,000 inhabitants, so many Jews, and so many Gentiles. Even the few Polish people living there also spoke Yiddish. That'll give you an idea what a city that was. And we also had a few Polish people, which went over to the Jewish religion. And they were all called Avram [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Because when they take our religion, their name is changed to Abraham. So they're all called Avrum [NON-ENGLISH]. You know he went over to the religion. Anyway, so it was established in the beginning of the 18th century. And in the beginning of the war, in 1939, it was next-- it went through some very stormy times, right? But tell me, Mr. Weinreich, is there anything that really--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes there's something which--

speaker: interviewer
text: --stands out in your memory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The people in Chrzanow were pious, right, God fearing, industrious, right, and very hard working people, right? Now, OK, I don't need to read that because I can tell you all about Chrzanow without reading this. It was a city which lived and was breeding religion. The whole thing was religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were Hasidim, mostly were Hasidim. And also there are also some institutions which was a first in Poland. There was an institution which helped specifically pregnant women-- ladies, which could not afford food or could not afford the children because they were very religious, that every year, had a child, right? And so the city was intensely religious, intensely closed. Everybody knew one another. And, you know, even today, you find people from Chrzanow telling you who you are, and who your parents were, and who your grandparents are. They know you. And there was a tremendous bond. And there was a tremendous sweetness. And it went through you, you know? You were breathing with it. I used to go to-- So you asked me about the festivals. So first of all, I told you a little bit about-- what's the name-- about Friday night. Then Saturday-- Saturday morning was something incredible. The whole city was going davening. They went to prayers, the whole city. There was not a soul. You know, 90% were very religious. 10% were not so religious. But everybody went to prayers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now then, in our home, there was a tradition. In our home there was a tradition that we all went to grandfather for kiddush. The whole family went to we went to synagogue. He always brought some very poor people home for lunch, always. And there again. And my mother used to tell me, you know, when the boys were singing, people stood downstairs. Because we were living on the first floor, listening, how they were singing the Shabbos prayers because they had such beautiful voices. And they loved it. They love the melodies. They loved the songs. It was something which not only that you sang it because you had to sing it. They sang it because they loved singing it. It was a love. Till today, I've got a love for hazanot, till today. And so it was intensely, intensely religious, terribly warm hearted, very warm hearted. And they had all sorts of organizations to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: In my family, my own family, which I remember very well, which will give you some sort of an idea, my grandfather was a very tight man. He was tough, and he was tight. Grandmother Malka was just the opposite-- soft, wonderful, good hearted, you know? Not that he wasn't good hearted, but he was good. Her life was-- what did she do with Chrzanow? What was her role in Chrzanow? Except that she was looking after the children-- she had five children, you know, grandchildren, all that sort of thing. She used to give a nadn to a girl which didn't have any parents. What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: A nadn? She used to give a dowry. A girl which didn't have-- which was a [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, she didn't have a mother. She didn't have a father. Or she couldn't afford it. So she used to give them a nadn. She organized a flat where to live. She had tried to help the young men to get a job, or get into something, so he could make a living. And what I love most of all, that she made them at the wedding-- the [NON-ENGLISH]. So the [NON-ENGLISH] was in the synagogue opposite. And in our place was the reception.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now at the reception-- and grandfather used to-- I'll never ever forget that. Then grandfather used to say, make the reception at synagogue. Why do you have to make the reception here? You make a mess in the house with all the schnorrers coming because they used to-- all the schnorrers used to come. And she used to give them shoes, you know, give them shoes, and all. So she used to tell him, Simcha, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. A Jewish girl you have got to beautify. To make her beautiful, you can't send her there to have a reception. She's got to have a reception at home. Since she can't have a reception her home, then this is her home. And I remember these cousins very well because they were very boisterous. And when the guys, you know, especially the poor people, no relations. I mean, nobody was invited. They used to just come along from the synagogue, all right? They start to drink vodka. So they were very merry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, she gave them new shoes. And they couldn't wear it because they were pinching. And they would undo the shoes under the tables. And I used to go mix up the shoes under the tables. I remember this very well. [LAUGHS] So this is the type of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now my uncles? I absolutely adored. They were such wonderful characters. I mean, Bernard was always my favorite. He was jolly. He was a happy soul. He, he used to come-- when he used to go away out, he used to take a bus to go out to the city. And the bus was in the marketplace. I used to carry his little satchel for him. And so he used to tell me, you carry that little satchel, and I give you some lollies or chocolates or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he came, he got into the bus. And he stuck his head out the bus-- next time! But when he came back, he brought me bags full of lollies. You know, he was a character.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now Abys was very serene, very elegant, with glasses, gold glasses. And he was a scholar, you know? And he was not so jolly. But I still loved him. And I loved his wife, and I loved the children. And that was Abys.

speaker: interviewee
text: Itzik was only newly married before the war. And I liked him very much too. And of course, the other two uncles, I didn't know because one of them was in Israel, and one was in-- but-- but. You see, there's a big but in here. I was telling you that on Shabbos morning, I used to have that-- my grandfather used to have to have-- [NON-ENGLISH], you know, to teach, to listen what I learned in cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every Shabbos morning, you know, he used to tell me, I got a letter from a nachem from Palestine. And he's begging us all, he said, my dear father, sell everything. There's a war coming in here. Big clouds are coming over Poland, over Europe. Sell everything. Get out. Come to Palestine. And so I said, one day I plucked up the courage. He said, so why don't you do it? Your son is asking you every week, that there's going to be a war, that Hitler is in Berlin, right? And there's going to be such a shocking war. So why don't you do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: On that he answered me, I went with these letters to my rabbe. And he was the hasid of the [NON-ENGLISH]. His name was Halbrarstam. I got a photo of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us his surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Halbrarstam.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-A-L-B-R-A-R-S-T-A-M Halbrarstam.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandfather met with him frequently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very. Because you see, what happens is in Poland, before the war, the rabbis used to travel. And they traveled from city to city. And he was-- the Halbrarstam-- my grandfather was a great-- he used to donate a lot of money to that movement, to the [NON-ENGLISH] movement. The rabbi was called the [NON-ENGLISH], because he came from a city called [NON-ENGLISH]. And he used to donate that money. So when he came to our city, he used to actually live in our place-- in my grandfather's place. And there was such a commotion at that time that I can't even tell you because usually they all went to the synagogue together. So the synagogue was packed to the rafters because the [NON-ENGLISH] has arrived. He was equivalent, as you would say now, to the Lubavitcher rabbi, but of course, with a smaller congregation. But this is the authority he had. And I remember very fondly that after Shabbos, after lunch-- you know, came home for lunch to my grandfather-- you couldn't go in. There was food on the tables. It was everything. And my grandfather was sitting next to the rabbi. And the rabbi was dispensing words of wisdom from the Torah. And I used to sit on grandfather's knees. And we each Chaim, from [NON-ENGLISH] or whatever it was there, by hand, with hand. I remember that as today. And all my cousins were very jealous of me because I was the only one which was sitting on grandfather's knees next to the rabbi. And the rabbi once even blessed me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have very fond memories of your home life and the religious life that you had.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very fondly. I had a wonderful, you know, youth. I mean, not youth, but childhood-- wonderful childhood. Smothered in love and affection. Smothered, from my mother, my father, my brother. Because I was a little boychik. And my brother was the big boy. And, and my grandparents-- and my grandparents, both of them, Simcha and Malka, and my uncles, and my aunties, and my cousins. I have got such wonderful memories on those time from, from the time I was born, till the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else can I tell you about Chrzanow? You know, the holidays-- Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, Shavis, Pesach. Pesach when it came, Pesach, I remember very well. Of course, Pesach was the beginning of spring, so everybody got dressed. Everybody got new clothes, from top to bottom. And of course, we went to the-- and grandfather had the seder, and the whole family.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a huge family, and also friends. And I remember just the shavirim, which I was in my grandfather's place, extremely well, extremely well. And then Rosh Hashanah was so solemn. Yom Kippur was so solemn. And it was just unbelievable. The Yom Tovim in Chrzanow was something incredible. And you know, also Shabbos afternoon, the whole city had a bit of a [NON-ENGLISH] for one hour, an hour and a half. And they all walked now [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is they used to walk-- go for walk after Shabbos. You know, it was their relaxation, whole families together was walking. Reb Simcha, Reb Moishe, Reb This, Reb That. And they greeted each other in the streets. And there was the main thoroughfare in Chrzanow, where there was the better part of Chrzanow, where the nicer homes were. My grandfather had a villa there. But it's beside the point. And all the people used to walk in there. And so I remember that Shabbatanz and the Yom Tovim. It was so deep in my psyche, inside me, that it's never left me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll just take a break there now.
